scenario: Non-Serious AE — consumer self-reports mild, transient myalgia on CholoClear-X; no hospitalization, recovered. Routes to PV via Gate 4; 90-day NON-serious clock. Proves Serious-vs-Non-Serious classification and the consumer-reporter path (Gate 6 fails as expected).
input_text:
SOURCE CHANNEL: Email Inbound Gateway
PRIMARY DISPATCH LOG: patient-support@cardiomedglobal.com

MESSAGE BODY:
Hello,

I started taking CholoClear-X about a week ago for my cholesterol. I've noticed some mild muscle aches in my legs and shoulders — nothing severe, I haven't seen a doctor or gone to hospital, and the aches mostly went away after a couple of days. I just wanted to let you know in case it's a known side effect. I'm still taking the medication.

Thanks,
Diane Foster
Personal email: diane.foster88@gmail.com
Mobile: 503-555-0173

failed_gates: Gate 4: Adverse-event policy — AE flag set -> PV (never auto-approve)
Gate 6: HCP link — consumer reporter not in HCP registry (expected for a consumer)

labeling_notes: NON-SERIOUS: mild, transient myalgia, no hospitalization, self-resolved, no medical intervention -> none of the ICH serious criteria met. This is the counterpart to the Serious cases (008/021/025/028) and exercises the 90-day non-serious reporting clock vs the 15-day serious clock.

CONSUMER PATH: reporter is the patient (Consumer). Patient is stored as initials only ('D.F.'), never the full name. Gate 6 fails because consumers are not in the HCP registry — expected and not a blocker; Gate 4 still routes the AE to PV.
COMPLETE ICSR: patient (self), reporter, product, and event are all present -> all four pillars True. Seriousness only affects PV triage urgency, not routing.
